content.text: Why Failure Mode Analysis Belongs in Journey Mapping

Conversational journey maps often end at the happy path. Failure mode analysis forces the team to complete the map by systematically enumerating every way each stage can go wrong and documenting a proportionate mitigation before development begins. In traditional software, failure modes are discovered through QA. In agentic systems, the failure space is too large and too context-dependent for QA alone to cover — failures must be anticipated at design time, not discovered in production.

The term "failure mode" here means any agent behaviour that causes the user's job to remain incomplete or causes harm. This includes silent failures (the agent confidently returns a wrong answer), noisy failures (the agent crashes with an error message), and experience failures (the agent technically succeeds but the interaction feels frustrating or unsafe). All three matter.
The Four Failure Categories

Failures in conversational AI systems cluster into four categories that map to the four phases of any agent turn: reading the input, processing the input, generating the output, and recovering when something goes wrong.

attrs.alt: Failure Mode Taxonomy for Agentic Conversations
attrs.title: Failure Mode Taxonomy

content.text: Input failures occur when the agent misreads the user's intent. Processing failures occur inside the agent's reasoning and tool-use layer. Output failures occur when the agent generates a response that is wrong, harmful, or unreadable. Recovery failures occur when the agent's attempt to handle a prior failure makes the situation worse.
Input Failure Modes
The most common input failures in production agentic systems are:

Missing slot. The user's utterance does not contain a required parameter (date, entity name, quantity). The agent must either ask a clarification question or make an assumption. Asking is safer; assuming silently is a trust-destroying failure pattern when the assumption is wrong.

Out-of-domain input. The user asks about something the agent was not designed to handle. The failure here is often not the out-of-domain request itself but the agent's response to it — hallucinating an answer, silently ignoring the request, or routing to the wrong tool.

Ambiguous pronoun or reference. "Change it to next week" — what is "it"? Without correct coreference resolution, every slot-fill that follows is built on a wrong assumption. This failure is particularly insidious because it propagates silently through the rest of the dialogue.

Conflicting constraints. The user asks for something that cannot be satisfied simultaneously ("book a flight that arrives before 8 am and costs under £100 to Tokyo"). The agent must recognise and surface the contradiction rather than silently satisfying one constraint and dropping the other.

ASR transcription error (voice channels). The speech-to-text layer mis-transcribes a word, turning a valid intent into a nonsensical one. Unlike NLU ambiguity, this failure is invisible to the agent because the transcribed text appears well-formed.
Processing Failure Modes
Processing failures happen inside the agent's reasoning and tool-execution layer, invisible to the user until the output arrives.

Tool call timeout. The downstream API the agent depends on exceeds the response-time budget. If no timeout is set, the agent stalls; if the timeout fires without a fallback, the agent surfaces a raw error.

Hallucinated tool parameter. The agent generates a tool call with a parameter value it invented rather than extracted from the user's utterance or context. The tool may execute successfully on the invented value, returning a result that looks plausible but answers a different question than the user asked.

Context window overflow. For long multi-turn dialogues, the accumulated conversation history exceeds the model's context window. The agent silently drops the oldest turns, losing slot values and user preferences established early in the dialogue.

Reasoning loop. The agent enters a cycle where it repeatedly calls the same tool with the same parameters because the tool's response does not satisfy its stopping condition. Without a loop-detection guard, this can exhaust the token budget and produce no response.
Output Failure Modes
Output failures are the most visible to users and the most damaging to trust:

Factually wrong response. The agent states something false with apparent confidence. This is the failure mode users most associate with "AI hallucination" and the one that most rapidly destroys trust if it occurs in a high-stakes domain.

Unsafe content generation. The agent produces content that is harmful, offensive, or in violation of the system's safety policy — either through prompt injection, an unexpected edge case in the prompt, or a model regression.

PII leakage. The agent includes personally identifiable information from one user's context in a response intended for a different user, or surfaces PII from a tool response that was not intended to be shown in the UI.

Low-confidence answer presented confidently. The agent's internal reasoning is uncertain but the output phrasing is assertive. Users acting on a confident-sounding wrong answer cause more downstream harm than users acting on a clearly hedged one.
Unreadable formatting. The agent returns well-structured data in a format the UI cannot render — raw JSON in a chat bubble, Markdown in a voice interface, an oversized table on a mobile screen.
Recovery Failure Modes
Recovery failures are failures-of-failure-handling. They occur when the system's first error triggers a worse second error.
No escalation path. When the agent cannot resolve a request, it has no route to a human agent or alternative channel. The user is stuck in a dead end.
Infinite retry loop. The agent retries the same failed tool call indefinitely without a maximum retry count, consuming latency budget and user patience.
Context loss on handoff. When the conversation escalates to a human agent, the dialogue history is not transferred, forcing the user to repeat all their information to the human.

Technical error message surfaced to user. The agent surfaces a raw exception message ("404 Not Found", "NullPointerException") instead of a plain-language explanation. Users have no recourse because they cannot act on a technical error code.
Severity Classification
Not all failures deserve the same engineering response. Classify each failure by severity before assigning mitigations.
Cosmetic. The user is mildly annoyed but the task completes. Example: a response with minor grammatical errors. Mitigation: note for a future prompt polish sprint.
Functional. The task does not complete and the user must retry. Example: a tool timeout with no fallback. Mitigation: add retry logic and a fallback message.
Trust-destroying. The agent gives a confident wrong answer and the user acts on it. Example: hallucinated invoice status. Mitigation: add a confidence gate before the response is displayed.
Safety-critical. The failure creates legal, regulatory, or physical harm risk. Example: PII leakage, biased financial advice. Mitigation: hard content filter and legal review before launch.
Catastrophic. Irreversible damage — data deletion, large financial transaction, identity disclosure. Mitigation: hard block with human-in-the-loop confirmation; no automated execution permitted.
The Four Mitigation Tiers

Every identified failure mode should be assigned to the lowest mitigation tier that reliably handles it. Over-engineering mitigations wastes sprint capacity; under-engineering them ships dangerous systems.
Tier 1 — Prompt guardrail. The system prompt explicitly instructs the agent how to handle this failure type. Appropriate for cosmetic and some functional failures. Cost: low. Reliability: medium.

Tier 2 — Runtime classifier. A lightweight classifier (a separate model call or a rule-based filter) checks outputs or inputs before they reach the user. Appropriate for trust-destroying output failures like factual errors and PII. Cost: medium. Reliability: high.

Tier 3 — Human-in-the-loop. The failure triggers an escalation to a human reviewer before the agent's output is delivered. Appropriate for safety-critical failures in low-volume, high-stakes flows. Cost: high (human time). Reliability: very high.

Tier 4 — Hard block. The action or output is blocked entirely, regardless of context. Appropriate for catastrophic failures. The agent cannot be prompted, configured, or cajoled past a hard block. Cost: design-time decision. Reliability: absolute.
Conducting a Failure Mode Review Workshop

A failure mode review is a structured walk through every stage of the conversational journey map with the sole purpose of discovering what can go wrong. Run it as a 90-minute workshop after the happy-path map is complete.
For each stage column, ask: "What is the worst thing the agent could do here?" Record every answer without filtering. The goal at this step is breadth, not realism.
Classify each failure by category (input / processing / output / recovery) and severity. Discard cosmetic failures from the risk register — they are noted but do not block launch.
Assign a mitigation tier to each failure of severity Functional and above. Note the specific prompt instruction, classifier rule, or escalation trigger that implements the mitigation.

For each mitigation, draft at least one eval scenario — an input that would trigger the failure and an expected output that demonstrates correct handling. These scenarios go directly into the eval suite backlog.
Document the resulting risk register in the PRD appendix. Each row: failure ID, stage, category, severity, mitigation tier, mitigation description, eval scenario ID.
Failure Mode Register Template

ID     Stage        Category     Severity         Mitigation Tier  Description
-----  -----------  -----------  ---------------  ---------------  ----------------------------------
FM-01  Opening      Input        Functional       Tier 1           Prompt: ask for missing entity name
FM-02  Dialogue     Processing   Trust-destroying Tier 2           Classifier: flag low-confidence answers
FM-03  Dialogue     Processing   Functional       Tier 1           Prompt: max 3 retries then escalate
FM-04  Resolution   Output       Safety-critical  Tier 3           HITL: human reviews before PII display
FM-05  Close        Recovery     Functional       Tier 2           Classifier: detect frustration signal; offer handoff
FM-06  Dialogue     Processing   Catastrophic     Tier 4           Hard block: no automated fund transfers > £1k

Assign a unique ID to each failure mode. The ID is used to trace from the risk register through the eval scenario to the acceptance criterion in the sprint ticket, giving auditors a complete chain of evidence that every known failure was addressed.
Connecting Failure Modes to Eval Scenarios

Every failure mode of severity Functional or above must have at least one corresponding eval scenario in the test suite. The mapping is one-to-one: failure mode FM-02 produces eval scenario ES-02. This traceability is what enables the team to measure whether the mitigation is working — and to detect regressions when a prompt change inadvertently re-introduces a previously mitigated failure.

At launch, the eval suite must contain passing tests for all Functional and above failure modes. A launch-blocker criterion: zero open failures of severity Trust-destroying or above without an approved mitigation at Tier 2 or higher.
Failure Modes Versus Bugs: A Critical Distinction

A common misconception is that failure mode analysis duplicates what QA already does. The distinction is important: a bug is a deviation from a specified behaviour that already exists in the system. A failure mode is a risk to a behaviour that has not yet been specified or built. Failure mode analysis happens before implementation. QA happens after. The output of failure mode analysis is a set of acceptance criteria and eval scenarios that QA later verifies — not a duplicate of QA work, but its upstream input.

A second distinction: in deterministic software, the same input always produces the same bug. In agentic AI, the same input may produce a failure on one invocation and a correct result on another, due to model temperature, context-window state, or external tool responses. This non-determinism means failure modes in agentic systems must be described as probability distributions, not binary states. The mitigation question is not "will this failure occur?" but "at what rate, and is that rate acceptable?"
Failure Rate Thresholds and Acceptance Criteria
Severity classification alone is not sufficient for launch decisions. Each failure mode needs a numeric threshold: the maximum acceptable rate at which the failure can occur in production.

Severity          Acceptable failure rate (per 1 000 invocations)
----------------  -----------------------------------------------
Cosmetic          < 50    (users tolerate minor polish issues)
Functional        < 10    (task completion must be reliable)
Trust-destroying  < 1     (users stop using the product above this)
Safety-critical   < 0.1   (regulatory threshold may be even lower)
Catastrophic      0       (hard block; zero rate enforced by design)

These thresholds are starting points. The actual thresholds for a given system depend on the user population size, the stakes of the domain, and the regulatory environment. A consumer-facing chatbot and a medical diagnosis assistant will have very different tolerance curves. Document the thresholds in the PRD alongside the failure register so they can be referenced during eval design and launch approval.
Cascading Failures in Multi-Agent Systems

When an agentic system involves multiple sub-agents — an orchestrator delegating to a retrieval agent, a summariser, and a validator — failure modes can cascade. A hallucinated parameter from the orchestrator becomes an invalid query to the retrieval agent, which returns an empty result, which causes the summariser to generate content about nothing, which the validator incorrectly passes because it only checks format, not factual grounding. The user receives a confident, well-formatted, completely fictional answer.

Map cascading failures explicitly by annotating the agent-internals row with dependency arrows: retrieval depends on the orchestrator's parameter extraction; summarisation depends on retrieval returning a non-empty result. Each dependency is a cascade risk. For each dependency, add a failure branch: what happens if the upstream step returns a null, an error, or a low-confidence result?

The mitigation pattern for cascading failures is defensive composition: each sub-agent checks its input before processing and returns a structured error if the input is invalid, rather than attempting to process bad input and returning a plausible-looking bad result. Document this as a design constraint in the PRD: all sub-agents in this system must validate their inputs and return typed errors, not empty strings or default values, on validation failure.
Temporal Failure Modes: Degradation Over Time

Some failure modes do not manifest immediately but emerge as the system ages or as usage patterns shift. These temporal failure modes are among the hardest to catch in requirements-phase analysis, but the most expensive to fix after launch.

Distribution shift. The vocabulary and phrasing of real users diverges from the examples used during prompt design. Intents that were reliably classified during testing begin to misclassify as users phrase requests in ways the team did not anticipate. The failure mode looks like a sudden drop in intent classification accuracy months after launch.

Prompt injection via accumulated data. As the system accumulates user-generated content in its retrieval store, adversarial or accidental content can be retrieved and injected into the agent's context, steering its behaviour away from the intended system prompt. This failure is not present at launch but emerges gradually.

Model regression after provider updates. The foundation model is updated by the provider and the new version handles edge cases differently. Previously passing eval scenarios now fail. Without a continuous eval pipeline, this regression is discovered by users, not engineers.

Tool contract drift. A downstream API changes its response schema. The agent's tool-parsing logic expects the old schema and silently returns empty or incorrect values for the new fields. The failure is invisible until a user asks for information that depends on the changed fields.

Document temporal failure modes as monitoring requirements: each one implies a metric (classification accuracy, retrieval contamination rate, eval pass rate, tool response schema version) that must be tracked in the observability platform after launch. The journey map's failure analysis phase is where these monitoring requirements originate.
Worked Example: Failure Analysis for an HR Leave Request Agent

Consider an agent that allows employees to request leave, check their remaining balance, and view team availability. Here is a partial failure mode register from a requirements workshop for this system.

ID     Stage        Failure Description                                   Severity         Mitigation
-----  -----------  ---------------------------------------------------   ---------------  ----------------------------------
FM-01  Opening      User says 'I need time off' — no dates provided        Functional       Prompt: ask for start and end date
FM-02  Dialogue     Agent confuses annual leave with sick leave            Trust-destroying Classifier: confirm leave type before booking
FM-03  Dialogue     HR system API returns 429 rate-limit error             Functional       Retry with 2 s backoff; max 3 retries
FM-04  Resolution   Agent books leave on a public holiday (user error)     Functional       Prompt: display holiday list; ask for confirmation
FM-05  Resolution   Agent reveals another employee's remaining balance     Safety-critical  HITL: strict access control check before data display
FM-06  Dialogue     User types 'just cancel everything' — ambiguous scope  Trust-destroying Prompt: scope confirmation required before any cancellation
FM-07  Close        Leave booked but calendar invite not sent              Functional       Tier 2: post-booking check that calendar event exists

Each row in this register maps to an eval scenario. FM-02, for example, generates an eval scenario where the user's utterance mentions being ill but does not explicitly say "sick leave," and the expected agent behaviour is to ask a clarifying question about leave type before proceeding with a booking.
Using Failure Mode Analysis to Set Scope Boundaries

One of the most valuable — and often overlooked — outputs of failure mode analysis is scope reduction. When the team systematically analyses what can go wrong, they sometimes discover that a proposed feature's failure modes are so severe and so difficult to mitigate that the safest design decision is to not build that feature in the initial release.

For example, a team designing a customer support agent might initially plan to allow the agent to process refunds autonomously. Failure mode analysis reveals that FM-08 (agent processes a refund for the wrong order due to ambiguous order reference) is Safety-critical with a Catastrophic financial impact and requires a Tier 3 or Tier 4 mitigation. The mitigation cost — human review of every refund — eliminates the efficiency gain the feature was intended to provide. The team decides to scope the first release to "display refund status" rather than "process refunds," deferring autonomous refund processing to a future release after a human-in-the-loop workflow has been validated.

This is failure mode analysis functioning as a product strategy tool: it surfaces the hidden cost of features that appear simple on the surface and enables the team to make informed scope decisions grounded in risk, not optimism.
Integrating Failure Mode Analysis Into the Sprint Lifecycle
Failure mode analysis is not a one-time workshop artifact. It must be integrated into the sprint lifecycle:

Sprint planning. For every user story that touches conversation flow, the failure register is reviewed and new failure modes are added for any new capability being introduced. Stories that introduce Trust-destroying or above failure modes without a specified mitigation are not accepted into the sprint.

Prompt change review. Every prompt change must be accompanied by a review of the failure modes it addresses and the failure modes it might introduce. This is analogous to a security review for code changes in a security-sensitive system.

Retrospective. At the end of each sprint, any production incidents that involved a failure mode not previously documented in the register are added. The register grows toward completeness with each iteration.

Launch gate. The launch checklist includes: zero open failure modes of severity Trust-destroying or above without an approved Tier 2+ mitigation. The product owner and a designated AI safety reviewer both sign off on this checklist before the feature ships.
Prioritising the Failure Register: Heat Map Approach
A failure register with dozens of entries can be overwhelming. Use a heat map to surface which failures deserve immediate engineering effort and which can wait.

Plot each failure mode on a two-axis grid: likelihood on the horizontal axis (rare, occasional, frequent) and severity on the vertical axis (cosmetic through catastrophic). Failures in the top-right quadrant — frequent and severe — are sprint-one blockers. Failures in the bottom-left quadrant — rare and cosmetic — can be addressed in a polish sprint after launch. Failures along the diagonal require judgment: a frequent functional failure may warrant more attention than a rare trust-destroying one, depending on the user population and domain stakes.

Likelihood estimates during requirements-phase analysis are necessarily rough. Use three inputs to calibrate: (1) analogies from similar deployed systems in the team's experience, (2) results from any rapid prompt prototype testing done with real users during the lo-fi prototyping phase, and (3) domain-expert intuition about where users are most likely to stray from the happy path. Revisit likelihood estimates after the first two sprints of implementation, when eval data becomes available.
Documenting Failure Modes That Are Accepted Risks

Not every failure mode will be mitigated before launch. Some will be accepted as known risks, either because the mitigation cost exceeds the risk cost or because the failure rate is already below the acceptable threshold without any mitigation.

Accepted risks must be explicitly documented — not silently ignored. For each accepted risk, record: the failure mode ID, the justification for acceptance (e.g., "rate estimated at < 0.1 per 1,000 invocations based on prototype testing; below Cosmetic threshold"), the monitoring metric that will confirm the rate stays below threshold in production, and the threshold at which the accepted risk will be re-opened and mitigated (e.g., "if rate exceeds 5 per 1,000 in the first month, escalate to sprint priority").

This practice of explicit risk acceptance is what separates professional AI product development from "ship it and see." It demonstrates due diligence to stakeholders and regulators, and it creates a clear trigger for action if the risk materialises at a higher rate than anticipated.
Communicating Failure Analysis to Non-Technical Stakeholders

The failure mode register is technically dense. For executive and business stakeholder communication, translate the register into a one-page risk summary that uses plain language and maps failures to business outcomes rather than technical categories.

For example, instead of "FM-05: PII leakage from retrieval context (Safety-critical, Tier 3 mitigation required)", write: "Risk: the system could accidentally display one employee's personal leave balance to another employee. Business impact: potential HR policy violation and employee trust damage. Mitigation: access control check before every data display; human review required for sensitive data types. Status: mitigation designed; implementation in sprint 2."

This translation work — from technical failure register to business risk summary — is the product manager's responsibility during the requirements phase. It ensures that business stakeholders understand the risk landscape and can make informed go/no-go decisions at launch gates without needing to read a technical register.
Failure Mode Analysis as a Design Forcing Function

The deepest value of failure mode analysis during requirements is not the register itself — it is the design decisions the analysis forces. Every time the team discovers a high-severity failure with no clear mitigation below Tier 3, they face a design choice: change the feature design so the failure becomes structurally impossible, add the costly mitigation, or remove the feature from scope.

These forced design decisions almost always produce a better system. An agent that cannot autonomously process irreversible transactions because FM-06 was classified as Catastrophic is not a weaker agent — it is an agent whose scope was calibrated to what can be made reliably safe. The failure mode analysis did not constrain the product; it defined the product's safety envelope, within which the team can move fast because they know the boundaries.

Teams that skip failure mode analysis during requirements find the same boundaries later — but they find them in production, at user cost, rather than in a workshop, at planning cost. The analysis does not prevent the discovery of limitations; it front-loads it to the cheapest possible moment in the product lifecycle.
Failure Modes in Retrieval-Augmented Generation

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems introduce a distinct class of failure modes that do not exist in pure-generation agents. When the agent retrieves documents to ground its responses, every step in the retrieval pipeline is a new failure surface.

Empty retrieval. The vector store returns no results for the user's query because the relevant document was never indexed, the query embedding differs too much from the document embedding, or the similarity threshold is set too high. The agent must detect empty retrieval and respond appropriately — not by hallucinating an answer, but by acknowledging the knowledge gap.

Stale retrieval. The retrieved document was accurate when indexed but has since been superseded by a newer version. The agent presents outdated information as current. This failure is particularly dangerous in fast-moving domains like regulatory compliance, pricing, or medical guidelines.

Irrelevant retrieval. The top-ranked documents are semantically similar to the query but do not actually answer it. The agent uses them anyway and produces a plausible-sounding but tangentially relevant answer. This failure is hard to detect because the response looks coherent.

Contradictory retrieval. Two retrieved documents contradict each other on the key fact the user asked about. The agent must detect and surface the contradiction rather than silently picking one source and presenting it as definitive.

Each RAG failure mode requires a specific mitigation in the retrieval pipeline design: empty retrieval requires a "no results" handling branch; stale retrieval requires document freshness metadata and expiration checks; irrelevant retrieval requires a re-ranking step or a relevance threshold; contradictory retrieval requires a cross-document consistency check or an explicit uncertainty signal in the response.

Document these RAG-specific failure modes as a sub-section of the processing failures category in the register, using the same ID scheme and severity classification. They feed directly into the retrieval pipeline design requirements in the PRD.
Failure Mode Traceability to Test Cases

Every identified failure mode should trace directly to at least one test case or eval scenario. This traceability ensures that the failure mode register is not a shelf document but an active artifact that drives test coverage decisions. The linkage works in two directions: forward-tracing asks 'which test cases exercise this failure mode?', and backward-tracing asks 'which failure modes does this test case detect?'

When a failure mode has no corresponding test case, it is an untested risk. When a test case has no corresponding failure mode, it may be testing something that product stakeholders have never discussed or accepted. Both gaps are actionable: the first needs new test cases, the second needs either a new failure mode entry or justification for why the test case exists. Maintaining this matrix as part of the acceptance criteria for each sprint keeps coverage debt visible.
Forward trace: failure mode ID → list of test case IDs that exercise it
Backward trace: test case ID → failure mode IDs it covers
Coverage metric: percentage of P1 and P2 failure modes with at least one automated test
Gap report: failure modes flagged 'untested risk' surface in sprint planning as explicit debt
Regression linkage: when a new failure occurs in production, link it retroactively to its root failure mode and add a regression test
Failure Mode Analysis in Multi-Tenant Environments

Multi-tenant agentic AI deployments introduce a class of failure modes that single-tenant analyses miss: cross-tenant interference, tenant-specific prompt injection, and rate-limit starvation where one tenant's traffic degrades another's response quality. Journey mapping for multi-tenant systems must include tenant identity as an explicit variable in the failure mode register and analyse how each failure mode manifests differently across tenant tiers.

Isolation failures are particularly high-stakes. If a prompt from tenant A leaks into the context window for tenant B, the failure combines a data breach with an output quality failure. The mitigation requires both architectural isolation (separate context windows, tenant-scoped retrieval partitions) and runtime assertions that validate tenant identity before each LLM call. These mitigations should appear as explicit acceptance criteria derived from failure mode entries rather than ad-hoc engineering decisions.
Summary

Failure mode analysis is the discipline that turns a conversational journey map from a communication artifact into a risk management tool. By classifying failures across four categories, five severity levels, and four mitigation tiers — and tracing each failure to a specific eval scenario — teams build agentic systems whose failure behaviour is as carefully designed as their happy-path behaviour. The result is not a system that never fails, but a system whose failures are anticipated, bounded, and recoverable.
